data.title: First Financial: An Under-The-Radar Value Pick
data.description:
Like I said in my last piece, I've been using "years of consecutive dividend growth" as a kind of proxy for safety in my ongoing search for high quality bank stocks. Today, that search has brought me to Terre Haute, Indiana, which is the home of First Financial Corporation (NASDAQ:THFF).

As it happens, historical dividend data doesn't really capture the picture that well here - the bank transitioned to quarterly payouts a couple of years ago and has also distributed some special payments - but make no mistake, First Financial has been raising its regular dividend for well over thirty years now. Unsurprisingly, that consistency has also seen it outperform the wider regional bank space over the years:

On the earnings front, First Financial didn't have the best year in 2024, but there's a one-off factor that goes some way to explaining that. Analysts expect EPS to rebound nicely this year. Given the stock currently trades for $42.90, that would put the forward P/E at less than 8x.

Tariff drama means it probably would be wise not to take that print at face value, but if you were to base earnings on the bank's long-run average return on assets instead, then the normalized P/E would be about the same. This looks like your typical under-the-radar value pick, and I'm rating the shares a 'Buy'.

Going Over First Financial

First Financial operates around 80 branches across parts of Indiana, Illinois, Kentucky, Tennessee and Georgia. Total assets were only around $5.5 billion at the end of last year, so it is pretty small. The bank's roots are in Vigo County, Indiana. Unsurprisingly, that's also where its deposit market share seems to be strongest, landing at just over 50% according to FDIC data.

On that note, I would say that First Financial has a pretty strong funding profile. Interest-bearing transaction and non-interest-bearing demand deposits form the vast bulk of its liabilities, and it paid a blended rate of just 1.5% on these balances last year. Expressed relative to interest-earning assets, its overall cost of funding was around 1.84%. That was around 50bps lower than the average peer bank (meaning banks with $3-$10 billion in total assets), and this has been a consistent theme over the years:

On the asset side, First Financial does quite a bit more secured consumer lending than the average bank (automobile loans basically), with this accounting for over 15% of its loan book. Its earning-asset yield is about average but, given its funding position, its net interest margin was 3.71% last year. That was well-above the peer average (which was around 3%).

Credit quality is also something I think this bank can outperform on. Nonperforming credits were just 0.35% of total loans at the end of last year - around 25bps below the peer average.

Looking back, it's true that First Financial's loan losses have been above-average in recent years, but this requires a couple of points of clarification. Firstly, net charge-offs on their own don't tell the whole story. We also need to consider the yields on the loans being written. If they are high enough, it can compensate for a higher rate of losses. This appears to be the case here. In fact, First Financial's net loan yield (the gross rate less net charge-offs) has been consistently better than the peer group average.

The second point is that this bank held up very during the last financial crisis. Its annual net charge-off rate peaked at just 56bps in 2009, around 25% of the peer group average. Unsurprisingly, its shares also outperformed the market during that period:

Sure, this was a long time ago, but I still like to see how banks did in those years. Plus, the current CEO has been at the bank for decades. Before that, his father was the CEO. I think there is a good chance that the overall culture of this bank hasn't changed much over the years.

The Valuation

Like I said in the preamble, 2024 wasn't the best year for First Financial's bottom line. EPS fell over 20% to $4.00, equal to a 0.92% return on average assets. Somewhat amazingly, that was actually the bank's worst print in decades, including during the last financial crisis.

The one-off factor that I mentioned in the introduction was the acquisition of SimplyBank, which closed in the third quarter of last year. That added some non-core acquisition-related costs to last year's expenses bill, while it will also take a little time to extract the low-hanging synergies from the enlarged cost base (eliminating duplicate back-office functions, for example).

Looking ahead, analysts expect earnings to rebound this year. Current consensus has 2025 EPS at $5.52, almost 40% higher than last year. Of course, there is a lot of economic uncertainty in the air right now. If there is a downturn and credit costs rise across the industry, then bank stocks are going to see a wave of negative EPS revisions. I doubt First Financial would be shielded from that.

I am more interested in what its earnings look like across the economic cycle. Looking back, this bank has averaged around a 1.2% ROA over the past couple of decades. Based on today's balance sheet, that'd be the equivalent of roughly $5.60 per share in normalized earnings power, so basically the same as consensus 2025 EPS. This would put the stock's P/E at around 7.7x.

The bank also pays a $0.51 per share quarterly dividend, for a yield of roughly 4.8%. Because the payout ratio is quite modest (below 40% based on 2025 consensus EPS), there's enough retained earnings to fund organic growth, M&A and additional capital returns to shareholders (buybacks and special dividends).

I think today's buyer could be looking at a nice combination of strong earnings growth and an attractive dividend yield - and that would be more than enough to produce a double-digit annualized total return. More importantly, there's a margin of safety in the stock. If First Financial's earnings power remains subdued at a roughly 0.9% ROA, that's still equal to around $4 per share in net income, meaning the P/E would stay at a reasonable 10.8x.

Conclusion

I can see how First Financial has achieved the dividend record it has. This bank sources relatively cheap funding and seems to be pretty good at underwriting loans, too. Being as small and off the beaten track as it is, I can also see how the market may have completely glossed over its stock. These shares only trade for 10.8x 2024 EPS, and that was one of the bank's worst years in quite some time. Normalizing earnings plus a 4.8% dividend yield can drive strong returns from here. I rate the shares a 'Buy'.

data.title: Uncertainty And Surprise
data.description:
A short note was sent to clients Friday, providing some thoughts on the market volatility experienced over the last two days. Although the note was written before Friday's market action, our thoughts are not changed materially. More in depth commentary is expected in our soon-to-be completed quarterly investor letter.

The S&P 500 Index began the week by moving higher in the first three trading days. The positive return was rapidly reversed Thursday and Friday after President Trump's tariff announcement after the market close on Wednesday. Thursday and Friday saw the S&P 500 Index down a combined -10.81%. The magnitude of this decline over a two-day period has occurred four other times since 1953 and the red dots in the below chart note those points in time.

As it goes with the stock market, there are no certainties, and this week is an example. However, the above chart does show these type of market events tend to not be prolonged. On the other hand, when a rapid decline occurs, a V-shaped recovery generally materializes once the event that caused the decline is resolved. As we note in the commentary, in President Trump's first term, similar tariff issues impacted the market and the resolution unfolded over a multi-month time frame

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: RTX: Geopolitical Turmoil Offers Growth Opportunities
data.description:
Globally, geopolitical tensions are increasing in both Eastern Europe and Asia. Countries are responding by increasing their defense budgets. In this geopolitically turbulent environment, RTX Corp (NYSE:RTX) is positioning itself as a strong player to benefit from this. RTX was created in 2020 from the merger between Raytheon and United Technologies and now has 3 business segments: Raytheon (defense), Pratt & Whitney (aircraft engines) and Collins Aerospace (aviation systems). RTX therefore benefits from both the increase in defense spending and from civil aviation.

The share price of RTX is lagging behind its European counterparts. European defense stocks such as Rheinmetall, Leonardo and Thales have already risen considerably, with Rheinmetall at the top (+1311% over the past 5 years). RTX Corp's return of just 183% seems like a pittance. A good opportunity to take a closer look at the stock.

Geopolitical Tensions Offer Opportunities For RTX

The global defense market is in a strong cycle. Due to the war in Ukraine, tensions in Asia and the Middle East, countries are investing heavily in rearmament. In 2024, global defense spending will rise to $2.5 trillion, an increase of 7.4% compared to 2023. The US has advised Europe to invest more in defense. NATO countries such as Germany, the Netherlands and Poland are finally reaching the 2% GDP target. In Europe, defense spending increased significantly by 18%. RTX benefits from this with its Patriot and NASAMS missile systems, sensors, radars and air defense systems.

What makes RTX extra attractive is the increased internationalization of defense revenue, more than 44% of the defense backlog comes from foreign customers. Contracts with Germany, Sweden, Poland and Japan give confidence in RTX' defense systems. Raytheon’s growing production of Coyote drones and increased demand for the GEM-T missile system demonstrate that the product portfolio is responding to current threats.

The aerospace industry is also in a state of flux in the coming years. Collins Aerospace and Pratt & Whitney are responding with new engines such as the GTF Advantage and long-term MRO contracts. Boeing and Airbus expect to deliver thousands of new aircraft in the coming years. And RTX is involved in almost every new model through cockpit and system technologies or through engine sales.

The well-filled order book of $218 billion offers sufficient stability for the coming years.

The financial future of RTX is closely linked to geopolitical tensions, aviation and American policy. However, Donald Trump's new import duties are a limiting factor for the further growth of RTX. Import duties on steel and aluminum can significantly increase the prices of RTX systems. Countries can then opt for a European defense system. And that is partly reflected in the stock prices: European defense stocks are rising, while American stocks are falling.

I see European countries looking for European defense suppliers such as BAE Systems, MBDA or Saab. Especially if those competitors are less affected by expensive raw material prices. RTX has an advanced product range such as the Patriot missiles and radar systems that have virtually no competition. However, price does play a role. Countries such as France and Germany invest billions in their own defense industry that is supported by the EU. If American products become more expensive due to Trump's measures, Europe can accelerate that process.

The question is whether RTX can offset the higher costs by implementing cost savings. RTX has production and supply partners in Europe, but the majority of production remains American. Without countermeasures or exemptions, RTX could lose market share in Europe, and this could have a major impact on its defense results.

Strong Results and a Strengthening Balance Sheet

The 2024 results are strong with revenue growth of 9% to $80.8 billion. Excluding divestitures, revenue grew 11% organically. Adjusted EPS came in at $5.73, up 13% from 2023. Revenue growth was driven by growth in all 3 segments.

Total debt is around $38.7 billion. And with interest coverage of only 4x, I find the ratio a bit on the thin side. In recent years, debt has been reduced, which has improved interest coverage. Peers like Lockheed Martin have a better safety with a ratio of 7x. For RTX, I don't see this as a problem because the order book is very well filled ($218 billion). The outlook is also good. For the full year 2025, RTX expects free cash flow between $7-$7.5 billion. And the expected sale of Collins' Flight Control division to Safran for $1.8 billion provides further debt reduction.

Rising Dividend and Good Mix Between Value and Growth

RTX is not only interesting because of its growth, but also because of its dividend. The dividend has been structurally increased for years by an average of 6% per year. The dividend yield is now 1.9% and the dividend payout is around 40-50%. This gives RTX room to increase the dividend further.

In terms of stock valuation, RTX is slightly more expensive than its US competitors. Lockheed Martin seems more attractive, but earnings per share are expected to decline by 2025. Both General Dynamics and Northrop Grumman offer more attractive stock valuations and analysts expect slightly higher growth. This makes RTX less interesting, but one should not forget that RTX also generates revenue from commercial aviation. The defense segment may be held back by defense budget constraints. Although this is less relevant for commercial aviation. The large order book of Boeing and Airbus gives me great confidence in strong revenue growth for RTX in the coming years. RTX may be slightly more expensive in terms of stock valuation, but in my opinion it offers more stability than other US competitors.

Conclusion

In a world full of geopolitical tensions, RTX offers an attractive investment through a mix of growth and a growing dividend. The global geopolitical unrest has led to higher defense budgets and that is very favorable for defense stocks such as RTX. This is also reflected in the record order book and the good revenue growth. RTX also benefits from the growth in commercial aviation. Boeing and Airbus expect to build tens of thousands of aircraft, and that is good for RTX. The results are therefore strong and offer sufficient assurance if the defense division declines. The balance sheet has been strengthened in recent years and is on its way to reducing debt. With the sale of the Flight Control division, the debt ratios are further optimized. The dividend has been growing steadily for years, with an average of 6% per year. If we compare RTX with American peers, we see a slightly higher share valuation. And that can be explained by the extra assurance that the commercial aviation division offers. In the event of a sudden drop in defense spending, this branch still offers excellent growth. What concerns me is that over 44% of defense revenues are international. And with Donald Trump’s tariffs, this could mean that the prices of their defense systems will go up. This could lead to Europe developing their own systems. However, this is not a risk specific to RTX, but a risk for all US companies that trade internationally. Given that the outlook is positive, the balance sheet has improved, and the geopolitical climate is favorable for further growth, I give RTX a cautious “buy”.

data.title: The Macro Fallout From 'Liberation Day'
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

The size and scope of the tariff announcement overwhelm the trade actions implemented during the 2018 Trade War, bringing average U.S. tariff rates even higher than those seen during the Smoot–Hawley Tariff Act of 1930. As a result, heightened worries about economic disruption have erupted.

The tariffs represent the largest U.S. tax hike in modern history, and the impact on consumers could be severe. We estimate the resulting hit to U.S. GDP growth at around 2.4%, with more considerable fallout if trade partners retaliate. The odds of a U.S. recession are higher as a result.

Additionally, with roughly 6% of personal goods consumption tied to imports, the tariffs alone could boost inflation by as much as 1.4%. Core CPI could trend back toward 4% by year-end, reversing progress containing inflation and weighing on real purchasing power.

Slowing growth and rising inflation create an increasingly uneasy macro environment that resembles stagflation, even if it doesn't fully meet the definition. This mix only adds complexity to the policy outlook.

While markets are likely to remain risk-off in the near term, as the administration’s focus eventually pivots away from trade, progress on more growth-friendly policy items, such as tax cuts and deregulation, could steady market sentiment, enabling investors to look beyond the destructive trade headlines that dominated the past two months and culminated — maybe — with this week’s announcement.

For more on tariffs, recession prospects, and what it all means for markets and portfolios, read Liberation Day: Market uncertainty, global growth concerns, and U.S. recession fears.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Enbridge: Get In Now To Enjoy A Multi-Year Bull Run
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) has outperformed the S&P500 (SPY) (SPX) (IVV) (VOO) since my last update at the start of this year. It has been a safe haven, having generated a positive return as well amid broader equity market corrections:

Thesis

I am holding on to my bullish view on Enbridge after the release of its Q4 FY24 results. I think the stock is a good investment that is rather insulated from tariffs:

  1. EBITDA growth acceleration thesis is playing out nicely
  2. Tariffs on Canadian imports may impact prices but is unlikely to lead to a drop in volumes of crude oil transported
  3. Enbridge is reaping the rewards of a good deal to bolster its Gas Distribution and Storage Portfolio
  4. Valuations are reasonable
  5. Longer term technicals indicate a multi-year or even decadal run ahead
  6. Valuation multiple expansion without underlying upgrades to EBITDA can be a risk

As with last time, my analysis is focused on the operating business of Enbridge, which comes in before the claims of both equity shareholders and preferred shareholders (TSX:ENB.PR.V:CA) (TSX:ENB.PR.Y:CA) (TSX:ENB.PF.V:CA) (TSX:ENB.PF.U:CA) (TSX:ENB.PR.A:CA) (TSX:ENB.PF.E:CA) (TSX:ENB.PF.G:CA). In the final section of this article, I have stated my hierarchy of preference between all these securities.

EBITDA growth acceleration thesis is playing out nicely

My initiating coverage on Enbridge was entitled, "Enbridge: Set For EBITDA Growth Acceleration". And indeed, this seems to be playing out as Dec'24 cracked the CAD 4 billion mark:

Looking ahead, there are multiple longer term demand drivers such as the transition of coal to natural gas, increased Mexico exports and energy needs of data centers. Altogether, this amounts to a 14% natural gas demand CAGR over the next 15 years:

Enbridge is in a prime position to benefit from these trends, as it is the largest liquids transporter and one of the largest natural gas transporters in North America. Hence, I expect EBITDA growth to continue and likely surpass expectations, as it has done consistently for the past 2 years:

In recent quarters, Enbridge has invested heavily in some acquisitions (discussed more later), which has led to negative FCF. But now, I expect the EBITDA growth to flow through and create a gradual FCF rebound back to and above prior ~CAD 2 billion per quarter levels:

Tariffs on Canadian imports may impact prices, but is unlikely to lead to a drop in volumes of crude oil transported

Nearly 90% of Canada's crude oil exports go to the US. And thanks to its Mainline Network, Enbridge makes up 65% of all those US-bound Canadian oil exports. This map depicts the Mainline Network:

Naturally, these flows between Canada and the US brings about the question of tariffs. The good news is that whilst the tariffs may have an inflationary impact on the commodity prices, the demand is rather inelastic and hence the volumes are unlikely to change anytime soon as it is difficult for US companies to switch to domestic sources at scale. This makes Enbridge's operations more immune to the impact of tariffs, at least in the short to medium term. Here is what the CEO had to say about why he has not so concerned about tariffs:

We've got tariff concerns out there, but there's such a hard wiring of the energy system in North America. We just don't see that as a material impact. And I think given what we're seeing from customers, that's actually bearing out in reality, and we're going to see it happen on the investment side as well.

- CEO Gregory Ebel in the Q4 FY24 earnings call

The President of the Liquids Pipelines went into more detail:

A number of our customers have integrated business models and infrastructure, right. Production, refining. We built a whole business around that. Supply switching to U.S. refiners, much has been said and written about that recently, but generally, suboptimal, right. So these are must run systems and society depends on it, right. So we view the impact on volumes on our systems, I think to be negligible in a tariff situation, again, depending on the size of the tariff.

- Executive VP & President of the Liquids Pipelines Colin Gruending in the Q4 FY24 earnings call

Enbridge is reaping the rewards of a good deal to bolster its Gas Distribution and Storage Portfolio

In Sep'23 Enbridge announced an acquisition of 3 US gas utilities businesses from Dominion Energy (D). The transactions closed by Sep'24, making Enbridge have the largest scale of gas utility operations in North America.

Here's how it has and is still expected to scale up the Gas Distribution and Storage EBITDA:

We can estimate that the total EBITDA contribution from the 3 acquired assets to be 4100 - 1841 = 2259 CAD million. Based on this, I arrive at an EV/EBITDA transaction multiple of 8.76x:

This seems quite reasonable given that the transaction multiples paid for much smaller ($5-10 million EBITDA) downstream O&G businesses have been just a bit smaller at 8.2x. As you can see from the chart below, the larger the businesses, the higher the transaction multiples paid:

My point here is that I think Enbridge has by no means paid exorbitantly for the inorganic EBITDA boost.

Valuations are reasonable

At a 1-yr fwd EV/EBITDA of 12.6x, Enbridge is trading at an 8.4% premium vs its peers' median multiple of 11.6x. This premium is actually much lower than the typical 20.6% premium it has usually traded at vs peers:

So I think we can make a case for ENB stock to be relatively undervalued. The normal premium valuation vs its peers is arguably justified given Enbridge's larger scale.

Longer term technicals indicate a multi-year or even decadal run ahead

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of ENB vs SPX500

The chart below shows the yearly chart of ENB vs the S&P500. Every candlestick represents one annual period:

In the early 2000s, ENB vs SPX500 was in a powerful decadal uptrend. And the last 2010-2020 decade saw a pullback with weakening seller momentum. For the first half of the current 2020s decade, the ratio prices have consolidated and formed a base. This year, the running candle looks very bullish. Hence, I think we can anticipate a strong breakout leading to a multi-year or even decadal bullish run, outperforming the S&P500.

Valuation multiple expansion without underlying upgrades to EBITDA can be a risk

Since H2 FY24, it looks like ENB stock (orange line in the chart below) has been driven up mostly by an expansion in the valuation multiple (blue line in the chart below) since the EBITDA expectations have remained flat (grey line in the chart below):

I am usually wary of a rally driven purely by a multiples re-rating, as it tends to not be as sustainable without underlying growth in a fundamental driver such as EBITDA. So this is a risk I identify to the thesis; perhaps much of the EBITDA expectations is already priced in?

One good sign is that over the last month, there have been net upward revisions in EBITDA expectations for Enbridge in FY25 according to Wall St consensus estimates:

So I am not too concerned about the bull case being already priced in just yet.

Takeaway & Positioning

As expected, Enbridge has started to ramp up its EBITDA delivery, bolstered by recent acquisitions for which it has paid a good, reasonable price. The demand drivers are bright for the next 15 years due to megatrends of coal-to-gas energy transitions and the data center sector's growing energy needs. Due to its dominant position in North American O&G pipelines, Enbridge is well positioned to ride this wave. At least in the near to medium term, it is difficult for US customers to switch away from the Canadian O&G imports coming in due to a lack of scalable alternatives. Hence, Enbridge's volumes are likely to be relatively insulated from the impact of tariff wars.

ENB stock is also trading at a lower-than-usual premium to its peers. So its valuations seem reasonable. And I am excited by the longer-term technicals, which say that we may be on the cusp of a multi-year and perhaps decadal bull run of outperformance vs the S&P500.

The table below, hierarchy of preference between Enbridge's equity shares and various preferred shares from top to bottom:

I am most bullish on the equity share class, as I think this is the best way to play for uncapped upside. But amongst the preferred share classes, my approach is to opt for the ones with the highest yield. But my rating of 'Buy' applies to all of the aforementioned Enbridge securities.

A key risk to my bullish view is that EBITDA growth expectations may be priced in since the stock has rallied over the last 10 months primarily due to multiples expansion. However, I am not too concerned about this yet, as it looks like the company is still seeing net upward revisions in its EBITDA projections for this year.

Rating: 'Strong Buy' to all of the aforementioned Enbridge securities.

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lonza: Well Equipped In The U.S., Buy Confirmed
data.description:
Following our update on Novartis and Roche, in a challenging momentum, we are back to comment on Lonza Group AG (OTCPK: LZAGY, OTCPK:LZAGF). On April 2, US President Trump communicated a significant increase in tariffs on US imports. Starting April 9, 31% of US imports from Switzerland will be subject to tariffs. However, the US administration has exempted pharmaceutical products from this tariff regime. In our last update, Acceleration In Mid-Term Sales Growth, we supported Lonza's latest Vacaville acquisition, increasing our top-line sales and EBITDA expectations. This bolt-on M&A will also offer flexibility to Lonza ahead. Even if we assume that tariffs will be reduced from current levels in our baseline scenario, estimating the exact consequences for the industry is complex.

Why are we Positive?

  1. Starting with the macro view, while equities and sectors exposed to tariffs have already significantly underperformed, we believe the latest news is not still priced in, and the earnings outlook remains at risk. Defensive sectors as pharma and utilities could maintain leadership in the short term, while consumer staples may not be considered pure defensive play. Going to Lonza, the company recorded a solid margin beat in the Fiscal Year 2024 results. Fundamentals are strong, with supportive margins across core CDMO divisions, up by more than 200 basis points in H2. The company reported solid results in Small molecules, and despite the Biologics miss, there are ongoing contract discussions. Our confidence in the company delivering on its Fiscal Year 2025 margin targets has increased post results;
  2. Before the tariffs announcement, we explored the potential impact of USA tariffs on Lonza. So, we report that the company has a sizeable 34% North American top-line sales exposure, but it is well-balanced with costs at 37%. Last year, the company acquired Roche’s Vacaville facility for $1.2 billion in cash. This strategic transaction will enhance the company’s GEO presence in the growing US biologics contract manufacturing space. Adding the Vacaville facility might increase US-based mammalian capacities to approximately 60% from 43%. As such, US imports for Lonza might be considered immaterial. While it is unclear what the customers' sales volume evolution will be, Lonza is a potential beneficiary given the ample availability of USA industrial capacities should biotech or pharma clients have relocation needs. On a negative note, in our estimates, we continue to anticipate approximately 150 basis points of margin dilution for a 30% utilization rate of the facility (at the moment) and a lower product MIX due to Roche exclusively;
  3. In 2017, the company acquired Capsugel for $5.5 billion (15x LTM EBITDA). According to Bloomberg's press release, there is an exit process with a disposal at €2.5 billion. That day, Lonza shares fell 2.5%, representing a >CHF 1 billion move in market cap. This was aligned with the delta value vs. the buyside estimates. According to our calculation, Capsugel generated a cumulative free cash flow in the range of CHF 1.5-2.0 billion during the period. So, on a positive note, the company should be able to exit this endeavor as a zero-sum game.

Earnings Change and Valuation

Before proving our financials changes, in our base case scenario, we continue to assume that the global biologics market should grow at a double-digit pace in the future. Lonza has compelling long-term growth prospects, with a sales CAGR of 10% until 2030 (Fig 2). Indeed, the large molecule drugs market, predominantly biologics used in brain disease and immune-oncology, requires more biological mammalian and cell manufacturing capacities. Therefore, this is in favor of Lonza's contract manufacturing capabilities. The new Lonza management team is less risk-adverse towards incremental CAPEX expenditure, requiring significant customer commitments with a well-defined ROIC (above 30%). This provides earnings visibility for the company. We anticipate the group to increase sales by 10% in 2025 to CHF 8.5 billion, and we also see solid underlying margin development in 2025 with the margin “approaching 30%” (Fig 4), which already includes Vacaville's margin dilution. This is backed by strong margin deliveries in Biologics and Small molecules in H2.

Additionally, the CMDO industry supply/demand remains supportive, and Lonza might benefit from reshoring demand. Our estimates forecast a CORE EBITDA margin of 29.0%, translating into a 25% EPS growth in 2025. With better sales visibility and a more cautious approach in CAPEX, incorporating Lonza's technical guidance (Fig 3), our net profit reached CHF 1.1 billion, with an EPS of CHF 15.4.

Source: Lonza FY 2024 results - Fig 2

Fig 3

Fig 4

Going to the valuation, considering the latest acquisition, Lonza's net debt moved to CHF 4 billion, and applying an unchanged EV/EBITDA of 21x (aligned with its 5-year historical average), in our estimates, we derive an enterprise value of CHF 51.76 billion. Then, our equity value reached CHF 47.76 billion, with a target price of CHF 660 per share ($76.7 in ADR). Our buy is confirmed.

Risks

We have a longstanding coverage of Lonza, so we report the following downside risks: 1) higher CAPEX costs with higher start-up costs (we also add delay in project ramp-up), 2) FX changes even if they are already forecasted in our numbers, 3) lower-order books, 4) value-disruptive M&A, 5) lower sales evolution, 6) overcapacities in the biologics industry combined with short-term demand headwinds, 7) product failures, and 8) lower funding cuts from the industry. Uncertainties range from new medicines development to existing sales cannibalization. There is also a valuation risk given the company/industry high multiple.

Conclusion

In our view, the company is less exposed to tariff uncertainty, and its CAPEX investment, combined with growing end markets, will lead to supportive sales momentum. We did not incorporate higher market share in our estimates, and applying the company's 2025 guidance, we see a solid risk-reward set-up.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CVS Health: Undervalued, But The Road To Success Is Paved With Uncertainty
data.description:
CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) is a company I have held in my portfolio for a very long time, and have been buying more as it has been falling. Today, I have to rate the company as a BUY, based on their undervaluation and their potential for decent financial growth into the future after uncertainty surrounding the healthcare sector is eliminated. To understand my decision, we first go through the market overview, where I will point out some important things, to then analyze the fundamentals of the company through our 9 Pillar Analysis and finally calculate its intrinsic value through all four of our intrinsic valuation models.

Introduction

First things first, let's understand what a pharmacy such as CVS actually does. A CVS store consists of two parts: the front of the store, where you can buy general products such as beauty, hygiene, cleaning, etc, essentially the retail part. The second part of the store consists of the pharmacy itself, the part where customers ask for prescribed drugs.

Analysis

Market Overview

Knowing this, we can jump to analyzing CVS. The first thing I want to point out is that with the ongoing inflation, the front-store parts of pharmacies have had troubles competing with larger retailers such as Walmart or Target. As people become more weary of their spendings, rather than shopping at a pharmacy that has higher retail prices of everyday products, they shop at cheaper, larger retailers. This is one of the things that has been hurting CVS, as 32.60% of their revenue comes from retail products, and even though their revenue has been rising (as we will see further in the analysis), their profit margin has been falling dramatically, leading to a huge decrease in the net income.

On the pie chart above, you can see that 52.20% of the company's revenue comes from Pharmacy Services, with 29.50% coming from Health Care Benefits, equaling 81.70% of the company's total revenue. This 81.70% is the revenue from the second part of the store, which as well has been suffering due to rising drug prices, which have been lowering the company's margins.

Profitability (Pillar 6)

In fact, when we take a look at Pillar 6 from the 9 Pillar Analysis, the profitability of the company, we will see that it has been declining at a 5Y CAGR of 4.21%, from 2.68% in 2020, to only 1.24% in 2024, resulting in a drop of 53.73% through the past 5 years.

Furthermore, the ROIC has also been falling from 5.78% in 2020 to 3.19% in 2024, so even though the company's revenue might be growing, their net income would still be suffering.

Anyway, before properly moving to the 9 Pillar Analysis, let me highlight one thing that always retains my bullish sentiment regarding CVS. The company has its own PBM (Pharmacy Benefit Manager) business, Caremark. This business actually holds the biggest share of 34.00% in the PBM market in the US, far ahead of Cigna's and United Health's PBM's.

If you aren't familiar with what a PBM does, let me quickly explain: a PBM is essentially a third party between a drug company, an insurance company, and a pharmacy. They negotiate drug prices, determine which drugs will be covered, as well as set co-pays and criteria. Now the issue is that lately, PBMs have been shrinking reimbursement rates and pushing patients towards generic brands over name brands, a move that helps the PBMs but hurts the pharmacies.

To help you understand the size of this issue, the FDA now estimates that around 91.00% of prescriptions are written for generic brands. Though what I am trying to say is that as CVS owns its own PBM, it can drive prices up or down and therefore is in a much better position than, for example, Walgreens.

Another issue is that the FTC has filed a lawsuit against the three biggest PBM's, accusing them of "inflating drug prices for a $7.3 billion gain." This, on one hand, can benefit CVS's main chain but might overall hurt the company's revenue-generating abilities. On a different hand, though, it might increase the company's profit margins, pushing their EPS higher and increasing their value, as well as stock price.

Overall, the main reason for the steep stock price drop we have seen through the past 3 years is due both to falling profitability but mostly, in my eyes, the uncertainty surrounding the company and the overall healthcare sector.

9 Pillar Analysis

Now that we finally understand what is going on with the company, let's look at the 9 Pillars, and understand why, in my eyes, they are worth more than the price at which they are currently trading at. But first, let's get to understand this analysis model. It is composed of 9 individual pillars, with Pillars 1 and 2 showing the 5-year P/E and P/FCF ratios. Pillars 3, 4, and 5 shows the 5Y revenue, net income and FCF growth, respectively, with pillar 6 depicting the historical profitability and pillar 7 focusing on the company's share buybacks. Pillar 8 analyzes the company's dividend CAGR as well as yield and payout ratios, and Pillar 9 helps me understand whether a company generates enough cash flow to cover all its debt. Below you can see that CVS gets a rating of 7/9, with one of the pillars in the red being the profitability, which we have already covered.

Financial Growth (Pillars 3, 4, 5)

So now, let's take a look at the company's financial growth. Their revenue has grown by 38.35% over the past 5Y, from $267.91B in 2020 to $370.66B in 2024, accounting for a CAGR of 6.79%.

From this, you can see that the company is growing, and at a very decent rate, though looking at the net income, that has decreased from $7.18B in 2020 to only $4.61B in 2024. This accounts for a 5Y CAGR of - 11.40%, which, as we know, is due to the company more than halving its profit margin through this time period.

Finally, looking at the FCF, that has as well decreased from $13.43B in 2020, to $6.34B in 2024, decreasing at a CAGR of 14.79%. Now, something I love about CVS is that their FCF is consistently much higher than their net income, meaning that the company's cash is certainly being well managed.

Overall, you can see that yes, the company has been struggling. Its net income and FCF have been falling significantly, though its revenue is growing. So again, we are at the core of the issue: the declining profitability caused by growing retail competition and PBMs shrinking reimbursement rates. But let's figure out how CVS's remaining fundamentals are looking.

Debt (Pillar 9)

Before looking at their valuation, I certainly want to see if they generate enough FCF to cover all their LTL. Their LTL/ 5Y FCF - Dividends ratio is currently sitting at 2.22, which is quite decent, meaning that with their average 5Y FCF, they would be able to pay off their $92.70B in LTL within 11 years while paying out their dividend. Therefore, I believe that CVS is quite safe regarding this side of the story.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

Getting to the company's valuation, let's first check out their 5Y PE, which is sitting at 15.69. This is slightly lower than their current PE of 18.80, but what you have to understand is that the company has experienced a large decrease in EPS over the past 5 years, yet, the PE is nice and low, below 22.5, in my eyes pointing towards the company being fairly, if not undervalued, compared to the net income they currently bring in.

It's the same case with the P/FCF, which is currently sitting at only 8.54, again a little higher than the 5Y average of 7.60, but in my eyes pointing to undervaluation.

Intrinsic Value

Now to figure out with certainty if CVS is under or overvalued, we will put the company through four of our intrinsic valuation models, starting with the Graham's Formula.

Here I will plug in the 5Y EPS of $4.33 and, combining it with my predicted 5Y EPS CAGR of 6.33%, get an intrinsic value of $66.97, 2.42% lower than the current trading price of $68.63.

For the second valuation model, we will look to the DCF model, where I will take the 2024 FCF sitting at $6.33B, and based on that, calculate the FFCF using a predicted 10Y + CAGR of 6.30%. I will then discount that FFCF at a rate of 7.00% based on the 4.66% WACC of the company and get an intrinsic value of $85.78.

Now looking to the Multiples Valuation, I will take two of CVS's competitors, Cigna Group and Fresenius Medical Care, and average out their PE ratios to 26.98. This, when multiplied with the current EPS of CVS sitting at $3.66, will give us an intrinsic value of $98.74.

Finally, for the DDM, I will predict the future dividend CAGR at 4.00% and get an intrinsic value of $92.21

Now to get the ultimate intrinsic value, we will average out the 4 values we just calculated, and get $85.93, 25.53% higher than the current trading price of $68.45. If I were to apply a margin of safety of 10.00% I would get an acceptable buy price of $77.33, which supports my BUY rating.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I am rating CVS Health as a BUY. This decision is based on a few factors, of which the main one is the company's high undervaluation compared to the net income and FCF they bring in, even though it has decreased a lot over the past few years. My rating would be higher if there wasn't so much uncertainty surrounding the company's falling profitability and rising competition, but despite this, I remain bullish as I believe that with the company's growing revenue and overall healthy fundamentals, they are poised to do well in the future.

data.title: Mettler-Toledo: The Spoils Of Dominating A Niche
data.description:
Mettler-Toledo (NYSE:MTD) is an interesting company. I have followed them at a distance for a couple of years. Only this week – aided by the tariff rout – did I pull the trigger. This newest addition to my portfolio has, in my opinion, a decent moat. Their earlier valuation was not attractive, but that has been changing as the share price has gone nowhere, while earnings per share have continued to improve.

To the point: the stock price first crossed into four-digit territory during October 2020, a year that saw earnings of $25/share. The price was on its way to a peak of around $1,600 in late 2021. From there, the stock has retreated to $1,100 today, while last year's earnings grew to $41/share.

Valuation has “come in”, and I felt it was time to cycle some capital into MTD. The multiple is still high-ish, and only justifiable if the business is – like the multiple – above average.

And I am sure that it is.

Background:

MTD was born out of the combination of a scale and balances business with precision instrumentation concern. Their merger in the early 1990s proceeded well and the company became public in November 1997 (the stock finished trading that month at around $18).

Their core activity is the manufacturing and sale of specialized scales and balances for both life sciences and industrial customers. They also produce pipettes and titrators. In life sciences (or laboratory segment) it has a dominant position that comes from the quality of the products and therefore brand recognition. More importantly, their adjacent service organization and the high degree of integration into customer’s process makes the client catalog very sticky.

Following is their customer and geographic segments:

Their salesforce is global, just like their manufacturing footprint. In China, for example, they produce locally for the local market and export market as well, they are not immune to tariff pressures. But their salesforce is critical for distributing their rather complex equipment and their increasingly important related services contracts.

The service sales, currently about 25% of revenues is a high margin recurring source that offsets cyclicality in both laboratory – or R&D – capex and industrial cycles. And that share of revenue has been growing faster, reflecting both an emphasis on growing calibration, preventive maintenance, repair, etc. and also a higher wall being erected around their clients for protection against competition.

The same is true in their industrial segment. Here, too, scales are a key component of production and for or packaging and inspection. MTD has carved a nice niche for itself with a lead or number two position in their applications.

And here again, their value proposition is more wholistic than selling the tool. The relative complexity of the machines and the client’s need for regulatory compliance translates to recurring services. According to the company, they believe they have the largest installed base of weighting equipment globally.

And calibration is something clients can’t do. Either customers need - for regulatory compliance - an independent certificate for the service, or to have the know-how and specialized patterns for calibration (that no one buys because they are used only once or twice a year). Incidentally, the brand also benefits by higher reliability when services are done by company-trained personnel.

In summary, the company is a dominant player in laboratory scales and a relevant player in industrial (larger) equipment - mainly to food manufacturing plants. Their position in both niches is the result of quality product and a good reputation – both of which are supported by in-house sales and service teams. The services further entrench their position with customers, and create a recurring and high-margin revenue stream.

All of this sounds very nice, but we need to see it in the numbers. The difference between a moat and a no-moat business is revealed in the income statement.

Recent results:

This is what a moat looks like. Getting 60% gross margins in manufacturing is unusually good. For context (and respecting the wide gap in complexity and technological prowess), ASML's gross margins are not yet at the 60% level, despite the near monopoly their scanners enjoy.

The resulting operating margin of over 30% is also exceptional. Both are helped by growing service revenues, but their constant progress forward toward these levels also points to limited competitor success.

Looking farther back, to the IPO, we can see a long track-record of improving gross margins and EBITDA margins. Completing the picture of a moat-like structure:

Seeing such progression, it is not surprising that the stock is priced at a premium to the market. Which is deserved, MTD is definitely an above-average business.

Valuation:

Look again at the chart above before looking at the P/E chart below (both cover the complete trading history). The business has improved materially over time, especially since 2015 in terms of EBITDA margins.

The P/E multiple sort of followed the improvement but overshot in 2021 - boosted by seemingly unsatiable demand during the pandemic - and now the multiple has potentially over-corrected to the downside.

At current valuation, the multiple is almost exactly equal to their average since IPO. Despite the company significantly improving their position since then.

For Mettler-Toledo, no valuation discussion is complete without including capital allocation.

Capital allocation:

The company has focused on share repurchases as the avenue for returning value to shareholders. They have done so consistently and to the point that book value has long turned negative. A happy result of buybacks that is sometimes seen in low capital-intensive businesses, like Moody's. The fact that MTD is there and involved in manufacturing, is pretty remarkable.

Although I personally prefer a more opportunistic repurchase program (like, for example, Copart's) it is hard to argue with the results that MTD has achieved. They note their average cost for all "retired shares" at about $300.

The capital return program is expected to continue, and therefore so will the debt levels, which management manages within a target range of 1x to 2x EBITDA.

Weighting the risks:

Since I just mentioned debt, let's go there first. Operating income for 2024 was $1.2B, and free cash flow came in at $900 million. This makes $2B in debt a manageable amount, especially as coupons are very low (interest expense was $75 million last year). Importantly, the +$1B in services revenue is much less cyclical, and can be a source of cash to rely upon even during downturns.

R&D spend is the only defense against obsolescence. The company dedicates about 5% of revenues consistently to R&D. Although some competitors are larger, the amount of innovation and software-related upgrades to their equipment has proven good enough for growing market share, and price leadership. We can ask for no more than that.

China represents about 16% of sales. This number used to be closer to 20%, but China has been decelerating in life sciences and in R&D spend. This same issue has affected others, like Danaher (DHR), post-pandemic and is an industry-wide headwind.

In time, emerging markets, including China, will likely return to faster growth. As for manufacturing, it is too soon to know what effect trade wars will have. The one thing we know for certain, is that gross margins are healthy enough to absorb some impacts and, in any case, these would affect competitors as well.

Strong margins are also a source of defense against FX volatility. In particular, FX depreciation in emerging markets. This is somewhat offset by manufacturing sites outside developed markets providing a production cost/ payable offset to any loss in sales or accounts receivable due to FX.

All considered, I see EM markets as a positive for growth due to the maturity of US and European end markets. We just have to take some FX risk to be there.

Takeaway:

Mettler-Toledo, with their specialized equipment and superior delivery, shows the benefits that can accrue to the leader in a niche market. Their margin progression points to a very strong competitive position.

The downside is that niche markets are, by definition, limited in size. Faster growth has to come from expanding market share and expanding margins. Service offerings are helping on both counts.

For shareholders, the other growth lever comes in the form of share repurchases. As pictured above, the company has reduced share count by 25% since 2016; highlighting a reliable path to grow earnings per share consistently even within a relatively small addressable market.

data.title: Jobs: A Decent Job Report Flew Way Under The Radar
data.description:
The analysis below covers the Employment picture released on the first Friday of every month. While most of the attention goes to the Headline Report, it can be helpful to look at the details, revisions, and other reports to get a better gauge of what is really going on.

Current Trends

Did anyone even realize it was a jobs release day, or were people too distracted with the trade war and stock market carnage?

The Headline Report showed 228k jobs gained in March. The Household Survey was close, especially relative to history, with 201k jobs added.

Figure: 1 Primary Report vs Household Survey – Monthly

For the year, the Household Survey is still higher, but that is due only to the data anomaly in January.

Figure: 2 Primary Report vs Household Survey – Annual

Switching to the headline report…

The BLS publishes the data behind their Birth/Death assumptions (formation of new business). In March, there was a very minor decrease in jobs from new business.

Figure: 3 Primary Unadjusted Report With Birth Death Assumptions – Monthly

For the year, the birth death is now neutral.

Figure: 4 Primary Unadjusted Report With Birth Death Assumptions – Monthly

Digging Into the Headline Report

The 228k jobs number was accompanied by an increase in unemployment rate to 4.2%.

Figure: 5 Change by sector

Jobs by Category

When looking over the last 12-month trend, this month was mostly above trend, except for government. Surprisingly, the government numbers were actually positive.

Figure: 6 Current vs TTM

The table below shows a detailed breakdown of the numbers. The table also shows that the Government numbers were negative at a federal level (barely) and positive and a state and local level.

Figure: 7 Labor Market Detail

Revisions

The chart below shows how the jobs data has been revised. January and February were both revised lower.

Figure: 8 Revisions

Over the last twelve months, jobs have been revised down by about 23k per month.

Figure: 9 Revisions

More Detail in the Household Survey

Another level of detail in the Household report shows full-time vs part-time job holders. This month shows that most new jobs added were full-time jobs.

Figure: 10 Full Time vs Part Time

Historical Perspective

The chart below shows data going back to 1955.

Figure: 11 Historical Labor Market

The labor force participation rate is still well below the highs before the Global Financial Crisis. This month it increased slightly to 62.5%.

Figure: 12 Labor Market Distribution

Conclusion

Not sure anyone cares about the jobs report after what happened the last two days in the stock market, but this month was very average. 228k was slightly above trend, but not in a major way.

It will be interesting if/when the government job cuts start showing up in the report. It is also pretty much considered a lock that we are headed into a recession, which could also start showing up in the job numbers

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Trupanion: Growth Still Has A Long Runway
data.description:
Thesis Recap

I last wrote about Trupanion (NASDAQ:TRUP) with the thesis that it was a great business but needed to be downgraded based on valuation. Anxious pets and rising claims made me worried about whether the company could live up to its premium valuation at the time. Since then, the stock has fallen 15% after my rating downgrade, which was reasonably good timing. Now, I reiterate a hold rating with my $45 price target slightly increased from $40 as the company shows inflecting profitability and significant FCF growth.

Finally Turning Profitable

For many who have looked at this company's financial statements, they have questioned the value of growth as it historically did not result in profits. However, in Q4 of 2024 the company reported excellent adjusted operating income growth and positive net income,

Total revenue was $337.3 million, an increase of 14% compared to the fourth quarter of 2023.

Net income was $1.7 million, or $0.04 per basic and diluted share, compared to a net loss of $(2.2) million, or $(0.05) per basic and diluted share, in the fourth quarter of 2023.

Operating cash flow was $23.7 million and free cash flow was $21.8 million in the fourth quarter of 2024. This compared to operating cash flow of $17.5 million and free cash flow of $13.5 million in the fourth quarter of 2023.

With positive net income and growing free cash flow YoY by 61%, investors are finally seeing the past investments in growth bearing fruit. Every pet they insure has an expected IRR of 34% according to their investor presentation, with an adjusted operating margin per pet of 11.8%. Because they are heavily reinvesting into growth by acquiring new pets, the company appears to be historically unprofitable as these costs must be expensed on the income statement. I would argue every pet they insure is a net positive to the company, as the expected lifetime value of cash flows is NPV positive.

Profitability on a GAAP net income basis does not appear to be management's main priority. On their investor FAQ website, they claim that "increasing our intrinsic value consistently over 5 and 10 year spans is what drives our strategic thinking. Cash flow is our second consideration. Profitability is our third". This matches my initial analysis of the company as I believed they were pursuing a high growth strategy at the expense of current profitability. Therefore, GAAP net income figures arguably do a poor job of judging the increasing intrinsic value of the company based on the very high ROICs the company earns from insuring each pet.

A better way to judge this company's sustainability and earnings is by looking at free cash flow. For the full year 2024, free cash flow increased from $0.4 million in 2023 to $38.6 million. Trupanion is finally seeing their seeds grown to maturity and can harvest a growing stream of cash flow from their existing based of insured pets. With adequate capital surplus of $288 million, the company can strengthen the balance sheet and pay off long-term debts.

Retention continues to stay strong at 98.46% for Q4 2024 according to the presentation, highlighting their unique value proposition in the market is arguably hard to replicate. This is despite seeing ARPUs go up 13% YoY, suggesting that customers are willing to pay up to protect their beloved pets. A high degree of customer switching costs and competitive pricing allows Trupanion to guide consistent growth, targeting 14% revenue growth in 2025 at around $1.4 billion in sales.

In sum, I think Trupanion can still grow and create value. Long term believers would be best advised to continue holding on through the ups and downs, as the company still has an unsaturated market to penetrate. Free cash flows are starting to roll in, suggesting their past focus on growth is finally starting to pay off. For me, I'd be interested in buying at a discount to my updated fair value of $45 if shares drop below $30. Until then, Trupanion is rated a hold, as most of their positives are closed to being priced in now.

International Markets Offer Opportunity

Trupanion still has a long way to grow. Specifically, international markets still offer plenty of business to acquire, which adds to intrinsic value as the IRR of each pet added is allegedly 34% according to the company's investor presentation. According to the earnings call,

Building on this foundation, we took a significant stride forward during the year with the launch of our veterinary first strategy in international markets with the introduction of the Trupanion brand in Europe.

Pet parents in Germany and Switzerland, which is home to approximately 12,000 veterinary hospitals, will now have access to Trupanion's high value product and unreplicated vet direct payment solution.

Building up the Trupanion brand arguably helps reduce search costs and gives the company strong recognition for its industry-leading value proposition. By targeting a 71% payout ratio to paying veterinary invoices, while covering unexpected illnesses and injuries that traditional pet insurers typically exclude. Trupanion's value chain of activities all synergize well to provide a long-term competitive advantage, building their proprietary data that can better price risks among pets.

Alongside their core Trupanion pet insurance, the company is adding other subscription products such as PetExpert and Smart Paws, which are just getting started. In Germany and Switzerland, Trupanion started in Q3 of 2024 and has added gross new pets of 5,500. In my view, this is early days for the company's long term revenue growth, and I expect sales growth to remain within 10-20% CAGR for the next few years. Their IRR for Q4 pet acquisition spending was 32%, in the range of their target of 30-40%. Overall, I continue to believe that growth increases the long term intrinsic value of this company, and that GAAP net income figures do not fairly portray Trupanion's potential.

Valuation - $45 Fair Value

I'm slightly adjusting my fair value estimate up from $40 to $45 to account for positive fundamentals and inflecting free cash flows. I continue to believe the story is more or less the same, but what's nice is that free cash flow is finally beginning to accelerate. Using management's guidance from their presentation, they expect adjusted operating income to reach $120-$140 million in FY 2025.

Taking the midpoint of $130 million in adjusted operating income, I will apply a fair multiple of 15x to this profitability figure. I agree with management when they tell their investors to focus on this number, as it represents the cash generated before acquiring new pets. Before reinvesting in growth, I believe this company is already current profitable from its existing pet base. My 15x multiple is to account for Trupanion's high retention rate and consistent high-quality growth rates. Therefore, multiplying $130 million by 15 gets me $1.95 billion in market cap. Divide by shares outstanding of 42 million gets me a $45 fair value estimate, rounded down.

As mentioned before, I do not believe book value is a good representation of this company's intrinsic value. Their significant reinvestments on the income statement in the form of expenses dents their GAAP profitability, causing book value to be very low. Their growth prospects and unique value proposition can't be capitalized on the balance sheet, leading me to believe P/B ratios here are not a good indicator of Trupanion's value.

Risks

Critics have said for a long time that this company is forever unprofitable. With short interest at nearly 20%, bears are likely skeptical of this company's growth as it has historically translated into no GAAP profits. They may further question management's IRR calculation, suggesting it is potentially overstating the pet's lifetime value, as it is hard to predict when a pet will die. And finally, skeptics may disagree with this company classifying itself as a subscription business instead of a traditional insurance company.

With this in mind, investors who take the bearish view may find that Trupanion is not worth much at all. Each to their own, but I view management's clarification and explanation of their financials to have already disproven the bear case. Investors who overweight a company's GAAP net income figures will likely find this company overvalued and continue to doubt its long-term potential.

Regulations and new competition could potentially stifle this company's target growth rate of 20-30% long term sales growth. Past growth trends do not guarantee future growth rates, so it may slow down as people assess different options in the pet insurance market.

Hold Trupanion

I still like this company, and at the right price of $30 or lower it may be another buying opportunity. Due to inflecting profitability and free cash flow growth, I've raised my price target from $40 to $45. Trupanion's pet acquisition growth strategy continues to yield high ROICs based off of 30+% IRRs, and management continues to expense these growth reinvestments on their income statement. Therefore, I reiterate that GAAP net income figures arguably do not fairly portray Trupanion's intrinsic value potential. Shares are rated a hold for now, with a buy zone recommendation of $30 or lower.
